Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
In the next hour we’re going to be focusing on something that can bring tremendous value
to you whether you’re a business owner or a copywriter.
The goal here for copywriters on this call is to add something to your tool box that gives
you the opportunity and the power to multiply your income. If you’re business owners as
we’re about to see, these tools can multiply your sales revenues every year many times
over.
Having said that, let’s get going.
The Makepeace Marketing Multiplier is just a fancy name for the strategies I use to market
to a house file. When you’re thinking about a customer file, a list of your active customers
– and we’ll define active in a moment because it can be different for different kinds of
companies – when you’re creating your strategy to sell things the second, third, fourth, fifth
time and on down the line to your active customers, there are only three things that you
really have to do to maximize revenues from your house file.
The first one is to systematically multiply the number of times each of those customers
buys from you.
The second one is to systematically multiply the amount each customer spends with
you each time.
The third thing is to systematically increase the time during which each customer buys
from you.
Some of the companies you’ll run into will contact customers maybe once a month or once
a quarter with an offer, so systematically multiplying the number of times each customer
buys from you basically means systematically multiplying the number of times you contact
the customer.
A lot of people who haven’t tried this, who haven’t gotten involved with this will tell you,
“Oh, we don’t want to bother our customers. We don’t want to contact them too often
because it will turn them off.”
The fact of the matter is that people love to spend money and if you’re contacting them
with good offers on good products that bring value to their lives, then they want to hear
from you.
We actually ran tests on this at Blanchard. When I first went to work there selling gold and
silver coins in the 1980’s, he was contacting his customer file once a month. I immediately
doubled it to twice a month.
Over the next three months or so we went to four times a month. Within a year we were
•
•
•

-- 1 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
contacting the list every single week and we were slicing and dicing the list so we had up
to 17 promotions going out at one time.
After a while of doing that we polled the file and we also looked at our numbers. We found
that the increase in contacts actually was increasing response rate, increasing the average
unit of sale, and causing our customers to stay with us longer, so anyone that tells you that
contacting customers regularly or frequently will burn out the file probably has no data to
back them up.
These are the three steps that we’re going to go with today. We’re going to talk about how
to do these things and how to do it in ways that actually increase response and increase
average sale with every promotion.
Let’s take a look at what this means when we increase each one of these three things and
why we call this the Marketing Multiplier.
If you increase the number of purchases a customer makes per year, you multiply the
annual sales and customer lifetime value.
On this table here we can see a company that let’s say for just purposes of our discussion
today, they’re doing two or three or four promotions per year. The average customer
makes maybe one purchase a year with an average sale of $100.00. That means the total
annual revenue per customer is only $100.00. If the customer stays with us four years, his
lifetime value then is $400.00.
Now you can see how that lifetime value and annual revenues increase as the number of
purchases per year increase. This is not rocket science. This is not brain science or rocket
surgery. This is just very simple arithmetic.
Then let’s see what happens when we take the next step. Here we’ve gotten to the
point where our average customer is making three purchases per year. Now we want to
increase the average sale of these customers.
Look what happens to our annual revenue. Remember we started with $100.00 annual
revenue per customer. Now we’re up to $300.00, $450.00, $600.00, depending on how
high we’re able to raise this average sale.
Look what’s happened here to our lifetime value. It’s gone from $400.00 to $1,200.00 at
the bottom rung, all the way up to $4,200.00 at the top rung, so there’s a multiplying effect
going on here. It’s not a one for one increase.
The next step is pretty impressive too. This is where the rubber meets the road in terms of
the value of the company.

-- 2 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page 3 of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
If you go to a company owner and his company is doing $3 million in sales a year for
example, because he’s got 30,000 customers, each one of which is buying $100.00 worth
of product a year and only staying four years, if you tell him that we can take him to these
kinds of lifetime values – remember the original lifetime value I think was $1,600.00 or
something like that – now we’re up to $5,400.00 if we can keep him with us for nine years.
The next chart really brings this into focus.
Before in our little example here, we had revenues per customer of $100.00. In a company
with 30,000 customers that’s $3 million a year in sales.
After working to increase average sales, frequency of sales, and lifetime value, we’re now
up to $600.00 per customer revenue per year. With 30,000 customers we now have an
$18 million dollar company.
That’s a six fold increase. We only increased buying frequency by 200%. We increased
average sale by 100%. That multiplied our annual revenues by 600%. Incremental
changes mean a lot.
But look what happens with our lifetime value. Before our average lifetime value was
$400.00. Remember they bought $100.00 worth of product per year and they were only
buying for four years. At 30,000 customers that’s $12 million dollars in total lifetime value
per customer.
Now by increasing our lifetime value to $4,200.00 we now have a $126 million dollar
company.
Again, incremental changes: a 200% increase in buying frequency, 100% increase in
average order, and extending average lifetime to just 75% gave us a 950% increase in
total customer lifetime value. It increased the value of the company exponentially.
Now think about what that means to us. Some of the people on this call are business
owners. Some of us are copywriters. For the copywriters in the group, you all know that
I’m constantly nagging you to be more than copywriters. Be strategists, be marketing
consultants, acquire skills that allow you to bring more value to your customers.
If you’re a business owner in this thing, the benefit to you is clear. You just took your sales
up six fold in a year.
If you’re a copywriter or marketing consultant, let’s say you’re getting 10% of sales on
house file promotions. You get $1.8 million a year. That’s good income.

-- 3 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Now imagine setting one company up on this kind of a program, getting your $1.8 million
dollars a year going, then moving on. Now you’ve got a template. You’ve already done it
once. Getting another company or two or three companies on this, you can see how your
income will really skyrocket.
Again, this has been a huge source of income to me over the years being able to do this
kind of thing.
A lot of company owners you’re going to talk to are going to balk at 10% of sales. That’s
fine. You could take a slightly larger percent of the increase in sales. You know how much
you’re going to be able to increase that client’s company.
If you go in there and he’s got a great product and everything is going for him, but he
doesn’t have this kind of a house file strategy working, he has no clue as to what you’re
about to do for him.
So usually if you go in and say, “Look, I’ll do the work for free on the front end. All I want is
10% of sales,” a lot of company owners will jump at the chance.
Let me make one other point here. When you’re writing acquisition programs, let’s say
you’re writing a magalog to get new customers; it’s going to take you a month to write it if
you’re good and if you’re fast.
It’s going to take another two to three weeks to get it through graphics and get it designed.
It’s going to take another two or three weeks to get the thing printed. It’s going to take
another two weeks or so for them to know whether it’s a winner or not. Then it’s going to
take another three to four weeks for them to turn it around and get it back in the mail.
So you can often be looking at four to six months before the second mailing of your great
magalog happens. If you took an advance against royalties on the front end, even the
second mailing may not be large enough to generate royalties for you, so it could literally
be eight or nine months before royalties from a customer acquisition promo starts flowing
to you.
When you’re doing house file promotions, you’re writing stuff today that’s going to go out
to that house file tomorrow. There’s not going to be small incremental tests rolling out to
bigger tests, unless you’re working with a company with a gigantic database and customer
file, so what you’re writing today goes into the mail or into email tomorrow or next week or
the week after, instead of months from now, and can generate royalties right away.
In that kind of a situation I don’t charge advances. I’m doing a lot of work with Weiss
Research right now. I don’t charge them anything up front for my promotions to their
customer file because I write it today, it goes out by email tomorrow, and I know that on the

-- 4 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
15th of next month I’m going to get a royalty check for it.
So all of that just to say you can afford not to work on advances and just take a straight
10%, but if a client company balks at that and says, “Well, I don’t know. That’s a lot of
money,” there are other things that you can offer.
You can ask for 5% of sales and a stock option. The 5% of sales will get you income now
to pay your bills and the 5% stock option gives you a stake in the company. At some later
date we’re going to do a session just on deal making. There’s a lot I want to say about
structuring a stock option deal.
Another way to do it would be to ask for 5% of sales now and a 5% bonus at the end of
the year. The customer client only sees the 5% he’s going to have to fork over now.
Failing that, you can ask for a monthly retainer to cover your bills and a stock option or
performance bonus quarterly or at the end of the year.
The point here is that there’s almost always a way to make a deal. It just takes a little bit of
creativity and a little bit of patience and you can get to the kind of deal that will get you at
the end of the day a minimum of 10% of the sales that you produce.
After all, that’s only fair. If you were a commissioned face to face salesman, you’d probably
be making that kind of money anyway.
If you’re writing customer acquisition promotions and getting $50.00 per thousand in the
mail on a promotion that costs $500.00 per thousand and the customers mailing to break
even, you’re getting 10% of sales on customer acquisition promos anyway, so it’s a very
fair deal for all parties involved.
In our example we showed that you got the company to $18 million in sales. He’s got to
pay you $1.8 million of that. That still leaves a huge increase for the company owner, so
it’s a win-win all the way around.
By the way, smart marketing companies will pay more than 10%. Dan Rosenthal used to
structure deals where he got 30% of sales because he brought something to the table that
the company couldn’t possibly do internally. I don’t know if he’s still doing that.
So value what you can do. Value what you’re going to learn today. It really is the key to
dramatically increased income for you as a copywriter.
If you’re a business owner and you want to understand this but you don’t necessarily want
to do it yourself, finding a partner who knows how to do these things for you is worth its
weight in gold. We just established that if you can do these three things, that’s really the

-- 5 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
key to exponential growth. So what does it take to do those things?
Obviously it takes superior products. It takes products that will make your customers want
to come back to you over and over again.
It takes great offers, offers that customers find irresistible.
It takes great sales copy, which is one of the things that all of you are either already
producing or are well on your way to being able to produce.
It takes top notch customer service. When the customer ordered, he felt loved, he felt
cared for. The product came quickly. If there was a problem, it was handled quickly so that
the entire experience was positive.
After that first purchase, the one that made him a customer in the first place, it takes more
personal contact with these people.
Now they’re part of your family. Now they’re one of your friends and so you don’t speak
to them in the same way that you speak to a prospect on a cold mailing list that you just
rented. You know things about this customer that you didn’t know about that prospect.
We’ll get into that in a moment.
But being able to access that information is key to increasing the number of purchases,
increasing response rate, average sale, and therefore number of purchases and annual
revenues from each customer.
It requires more frequent contact as we already discussed. One way to get more orders is
to increase your response rates, which you can do through better copy, better offers, and
more personal contact.
The other way is through more frequent contact, making those contacts enjoyable for the
customer. I’ve actually had situations where customers wrote to us and asked where their
promotion piece was that month because they missed it.
If you do it right, we’ll cover that a little bit today, but we’re going to do it in depth later in a
webinar on how to talk to a customer. More frequent contact will also result in increased
sales.
Varied types of contact are a key to making those more frequent contacts palatable and
enjoyable for the customer.
Then there are great bonding strategies. We’re going to cover all of these in principle
today.

-- 6 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
The first step in setting up a program for a client company is really to define what an
active customer is. At most companies that I work with, we define an active customer as
somebody who has purchased a product from the company within the last 12 months.
Now that isn’t carved in stone anywhere. The fact of the matter is if you’re working with
let’s say a book club company; they have a different product cycle. They may consider a
customer dead in the water if he hasn’t ordered in 90 days.
But for most companies, and certainly with the gold coin companies I’ve worked with, the
newsletter publishers I’ve worked with, the health product buyers I’ve worked with, the
financial newsletter and health newsletter companies I’ve worked with, if a customer hasn’t
bought a product from you within 12 months, he pretty much expires. He’s handled in a
completely different way.
He’s still contacted as a customer, but if he hasn’t responded in 12 months, what does
that mean? It means he’s not as likely as a regular customer to respond to your next
promotion.
What does that mean? It means you can’t spend as much money to talk to him as you
can to a customer that buys from you every 30, 60 or 90 days, so they’re handled entirely
differently.
Step one if you’re going into a company is to figure out what you’re going to define as an
active customer. It may be somebody who’s bought at three months, six months, and 12
months – that’s pretty much the norm for the companies I work with.
Now each of those customers’ information is stored on a customer file. That’s just simply a
piece of software that keeps database records in it.
Some of those customers are going to be more valuable to the company than others.
You’re going to have non-buying leads and inquiries. Every company has people who
have written them letters or called and asked questions about the product but didn’t buy.
That’s the lowest level on our database here.
The next level is the expired customers: people who have bought in the past but who
have not purchased in 12 months or longer. At almost every company you’ll work with, this
group here, the expired customers, is going to be the biggest file he’s got because he’s
been in business for years.
Let’s say at Phillips Publishing for example. They had eight or ten financial newsletters.
They were publishing them for years. Each one had tens of thousands of subscribers, and
over the years tens of thousands of subscribers allowed their subscriptions to lapse. So

-- 7 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
they have this large group of expired customers.
This is a fantastic asset for a company to have because whenever you mail a customer
acquisition package this expired customer file is going to respond much higher than cold
rental files of people who don’t know you.
Then you can see the progression here. You’re going to have low frequency, low amount
buyers on your file who are active. From here up it’s active customers. You’re going to
have medium frequency, low amount buyers. You’re going to have medium frequency,
medium amount buyers and so forth, until you get to marketing nirvana here.
These are your high frequency, high amount buyers. They may buy from you one time a
month, two or three times a month, and each purchase is at the maximum amount that
you ask for in your promotion.
Your mission then is to systematically move buyers up through the ranks to get as many
of these guys to this level as possible and so forth. This would by definition reduce the
number of names at this end of the scale, increase the number of names at this end of the
scale, therefore increasing revenues and company value.
Okay, I think that’s pretty clear. Let’s move on.
Promotions crafted to upgrade selected file segments -- Remember in the previous slide
what we’re trying to do every month is upgrade portions of the file to make the file more
responsive and more valuable and to increase customer lifetime value, so promotions
crafted to do that, to upgrade these file segments, require that you be able to select
portions of the file by certain key criteria. Those criteria quite often will usually include
these things and sometimes others. We’ll look at that in depth in a moment.
Age - You want to be able to go to your file and say, “Okay, I notice that the people who
are between 40 and 50 are not buying our product at the same rate as people between 50
and 60.” So I may want to create a campaign to change that, to identify what the problem
is and then to address it, therefore moving all of these people up to the next level.
Or maybe it’s men versus women. I may want to select a group of people based on how
long they’ve been on my file.
Inner circle campaigns are one way to do this. “Hey, let’s take everyone that’s been with us
for five years or longer and do a campaign to them where they offer them everything we
produce at some un-godly high price.”
We’ll talk about that in a moment. We’ve done that successfully with financial products.

-- 8 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Product preferences - These guys in a supplement company, all of these guys love heart
products but none of them every buys an antioxidant product.
Well, let’s change that. That would require a campaign maybe that shows how antioxidants
promote heart health just as well as the dedicated heart products that we’ve offered in the
past.
Purchasing frequency is another selector that you may want to use. We’ll look at all of
these from here on down in more detail right now.
So now if you have that data, what can you do?
Here’s one way to use it. You could create a promotion and then you could say, “Let’s mail
this to just our mail customers, only the people that are 50 years or older, only people who
have been with us 12 months or more, only the people who have bought with us at least
three times a year and who’s average order is over $200.00 and who’ve spent a total of
$1,000.00 or more with us.”
I used this kind of a campaign at Blanchard and Company when we were introducing the
world’s first platinum coins that were just being released by the Swiss Mint. I was able
to say, “Okay guys, look. I want a list of everyone that’s been with me for three years or
more, who buys from us at least six times a year, whose average order is over $3,000.00
and who’s spent at least $50,000 with us.”
Now given that kind of fine detail, they came back with a mailing list of a couple of
thousand people.
I knew that these were our prime customers. These people love us to death. They also
love our products to death. I was able to confidently pack up these $3,500 platinum proof
sets and send one each to each customer on approval. We just asked them to return it if
they didn’t want it.
We didn’t have a single customer refuse to pay us for the set. We actually sold out the
entire mintage just with that one promotion. Every customer that received it either returned
the set and declined to order, or kept the set and ordered more. That’s the power of
knowing your customers well enough to do this kind of segmentation.
The idea here is that being able to match product offers with the customers who are most
likely to respond multiplies response and revenues from every segment of your file. It
automatically upgrades customer’s recency and total amount spent with your company. It
gives you enormous power.
Another way to use this kind of data would be in a promotion where we want to target

-- 9 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page 0 of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
groups that we want to upgrade. In the first case, we targeted groups that we knew were
most likely to buy this product.
In this kind of a promotion, you target them on the basis of this person has bought three
times a year average for the last four years and we want to upgrade him to four time
purchase. He’s right there. He’s bought three times this year, so if we can push him
over the line and break him through that mental or that emotional barrier and make that
additional fourth purchase, we will have upgraded him.
Most importantly, it comes to price point. This guy has never spent more than $399.00 with
us on a particular purchase. If we can get him to spend $425.00 on this next promotion,
we will have broken him through that emotional barrier and gotten him into the $400.00
range. That brings enormous value to the file. So that’s the second way of using this kind
of data.
The heart of it all is the customer record. This is the record that exists in database software
that your client has. If he doesn’t have, he’s going to need for you to be able to work
your magic with him. This is just a sample of the kind of information that would exist on a
customer record.
By the way, just to make this easy, you’re probably aware of Microsoft Excel. It’s just
a simple spreadsheet program. A lot of these database programs are little more than
glorified spreadsheet programs that are set up in a way that allow them to store this
information and then crunch the numbers and manipulate the information.
On this one here, this is Mr. Fred Flintstone. We have his address. We know where he
lives, his city, his state, his zip code.
Let’s just look at this for a moment. Let’s say in the online model our model is to generate
an ezine subscriber first and then to turn him into a customer.
So when he opted-in to our ezine, we knew his first name was Fred and we knew his
email address. All of these other fields were empty at that point. We didn’t know his middle
initial, his last name, his address, or any of this other stuff.
But since then Fred has bought something from us. When he made his first purchase, we
learned his middle initial, his last name, his address, his city, state, and zip code and his
phone number most likely, depending on how our shopping cart was set up.
So we learned all of this about him. The minute he purchases we know this material from
about here up.
Now the next step is to start fleshing out the rest of what we know about the guy. We’ll talk

-- 10 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
about his personal stuff in a minute.
We also now know that we acquired him on this date. We know that the source was a
package that Clayton Makepeace wrote. That’s what MP means and it was package
number seven that Clayton Makepeace wrote.
We know that it was a direct mail package, not an online promotion. We know that he
bought Safe Money Report, a newsletter. We know that he paid $99.00 and that he
ordered by telephone. Old Fred seems to like the telephone. He had an order form, a
response device, but he chose to call instead.
The activity code we’re going to cover in depth here next. Then in the next part of the
customer record you have personal information and where he came from.
One thing I didn’t put on here that is also a very good idea – after the source, the creative,
the direct mail piece, is what mailing list you found his name on. That would typically be a
code that you could look up.
There are so many mailing lists you’re using you probably wouldn’t want to put its whole
name in. You have a code that would tell you what mailing list he came from. That gives
you additional information in terms of what he bought in the past and so forth should you
want to check that out.
Then we have the activity code. This is also called a RFA code. We’re going to get into
depth on that.
Then you have his activity, his history. For every time he purchases from you, you’re going
to record when he bought and the source of the purchase. In this case it’s an email blast #
234. You sent him an email and he responded to that and bought the product promoted in
the email.
The contact mode in this case was email. He bought Real Wealth Report which is another
newsletter. He paid full boat, $189. Once again he called his order in rather than to buy it
on the shopping cart.
So this is a basic customer record. It gives you a lot of information about the customer.
In these days software has advanced to the point where you could typically tell somebody,
“I want everybody who became a customer prior to 2/29.” Your database people, your
IT people, would be able to generate a list for you. You just give them plain English
instructions and they can generate that list for you.
A lot of companies that don’t have that kind of sophistication rely very heavily on activity

-- 11 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
codes or RFA codes. These were first developed decades ago mostly by the fundraising
industry. They needed to know how active their donors were and how much they gave on
average.
I learned about them from fundraisers and began adapting them in 1982 to product sale
companies. It gave me amazing control over how I wanted to segment my customer file
each month for promotions. We’ll take a look at those in a minute.
I want to go back to this. If any of you have read Harvey McKay’s books you know about
the McKay 66. Harvey’s a character. Harvey spoke at our conference last year and he was
a hoot.
He came up with this idea that every time we have contact with a customer we’re going
to learn more about that customer. Knowing more about the customer makes us closer to
them and easier to make the next sale.
Harvey worked in face-to-face sales. His sales reps sold envelopes. He owned an
envelope company up in Minneapolis. If a salesman came back with a sale but didn’t
come back with an additional piece of information about the client, his commission wasn’t
as large. Harvey enforced that.
Over time he was able to build up a database on his customers where he knew so much
about them that they felt more like friends than customers. The sales rep then felt more
like a friend than a sales rep.
I’ve been adapting the same thing in direct response marketing. If it works face-to-face,
why wouldn’t it also work when you’re communicating with large groups of people?
So apply the McKay 66. Identify other things that if you know them about your customer
you can use them in your sales copy to that customer. Here are a few ideas.
You can find out what his education level was, where he went to school and what his
major was in college. You can find out what his hobbies are and, this is particularly
interesting, does he have a religious preference?
What is his spouse’s name? There’s nothing better on a customer file than being able to
send a reminder that your wife’s birthday is coming up. Spouse email address. “I really
shouldn’t be writing you but Fred really needs a little push from behind. He’s forgotten to
schedule your vacation this year.”
To have children’s names to use in a field in a personalized communication and birthdays.
Depending on what your product is you may find a completely different set of extended
customer information that is helpful.

-- 12 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page 3 of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
This gets back to copywriting. If the copywriter knows he has this kind of information
available to him when addressing a particular file segment, it will have a big impact on how
he writes the copy. He can personalize a letter to mention some of these things or key on
them.
It also gives you other ways you can segment the file. You can decide to only go to people
whose hobby is bowling. Maybe only people who have children or only people who have
daughters or whatever. It gives you tremendous power over your file.
The way to get that information is three fold.
The first thing I do when I’m working with a new client is to pull the file. There’s a lot that
I want to know about the file and I’m willing to bribe his customers to answer my little
survey.
It will typically be just four or five questions. At least one or two of those questions will be
carefully designed to add a key piece of information to his customer file that I can use in
later promotions.
Also it’s very important to task the telephone customer service reps to engage customers
in conversation so they can flesh out each customer’s record with every telephone
contact. That’s really the most natural time to do it.
I have a client that has 30 to 35 telephone sales reps. Every single time someone calls
in it needs to be like you’re calling a friend. It’s chatty, it’s friendly, and you find out where
they’re going on vacation. You find out what their hobbies are and you get to know more
about their lives. Immediately it gets added to their customer file.
The key here isn’t a nuts and bolts one. The key here is to create this overall feeling of
family and warmth. “We care about you, not just because you give us money but we care
about you as a person.” Gathering this information allows you to create those types of
promotions.
We talked about the activity code or the RFA code. In companies that don’t have a more
advanced data processing and IT departments, who are basically maintaining their mailing
lists themselves on a spreadsheet or whatever, you may want to use the RFA code. All it
contains is a series of digits.
Here’s one as an example that tells you five crucial things about each active customer.
These are the things you’re going to use most often to segment the file in order to upgrade
the customer and to select customers who are most likely to respond to a given offer.

-- 13 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
The first one is the recency code. In this case that’s a G. That is time since his last order.
He may have ordered 3 or 6 or nine months ago.
The second one is the frequency code. That’s the average number of orders he’s
placed per year during his lifetime. As we already discussed that’s an important piece of
information to have.
Let’s say he orders three times per year on average. It’s June and you find he’s ordered
three times this year. Is he going to be dead to you for the rest of the year, or are you
going to do a campaign to up his average number of orders per year in the months
ahead?
The next one is the Amount 1 code. That’s the amount of the average purchase over the
customer’s lifetime. That’s self explanatory.
Amount 2 is the amount of the largest purchase to date. He may have had a lot of $49 or
$29 orders. One time, though, he bought a $129 product from you. It tells you what his
mental or emotional ceiling has been so far in terms of buying from your company. Again,
it’s a very important number because you’ll want to upgrade that amount systematically.
Five is the total purchases to date, Amount 3. That allows you to select groups of people
who have bought the most from your company, your strongest customers, to make special
offers to them. We’ll look at what a couple of those offers might be in a moment.
Again, here’s recency. This guy is a G which means he’s bought within the last 181 to 210
days. This is just to show you how the codes are often set up. Each letter of the alphabet
has a date range.
You could use a 30 here, but that’s two digits. The reason they typically use letters instead
of numbers, rather than take room on the database for the extra digit, is they find using
alpha codes saves that. This is really a holdover from the old days when computer storage
was very expensive.
Here you can see how recency is. This guy is a G, so therefore his last order was within
the last 181to 210 days. That’s somewhere around 7 months.
Frequency code is a C. That means he averages three orders per year.
Amount 1 is the average purchase size. His is between $100 and $199.
Let me just say that if you’re setting this up for a client, you get to decide what these
ranges are. For example, because a client’s smallest purchase price is $49 then an A
customer would a $0 to $49 dollar customer or $1 to $49 customer. This is part of setting

-- 14 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
up the client’s house file marketing program.
Note that I just made all this up. This is arbitrary. This is driven more by what the price
points are of your client’s products. In this case anyway his average purchase size is
between $199 and $100.
You may also decide that you want these to be finer in terms of the ranges. You may want
smaller ranges to give you more control.
Amount 2 is the largest purchase to date. It’s the same deal. Amount 2, this guy’s largest
purchase, was somewhere in this range.
Amount 3 is total purchases to date. This guy has spent somewhere between this amount.
This range is typically set up by where you see the break points on your customer file
and you can adjust them from time to time. You may find the ranges are larger or smaller.
Think your way through it and you’ll get it right.
This is what you do with that information. The customer we just looked at, his last order
was about seven months ago. You can upgrade his buying frequency at this point.
He places an average of three orders per year. You can get him to buy four.
His average purchase size is between $100 and $199. You can send a promotion in to get
him to buy a product that costs more than $199.
His largest purchase to date is between $200 and $399. You can do the same thing here,
too, to break that barrier.
His total purchases to date are in this area. Everything you do to him is aimed at upgrading
him in this area.
That’s the use of RFA codes.
Once you have your database set up, you’re going to want your client to produce a
report for you. That report is going to have counts for each of the items in your recency,
frequency, and amounts codes.
This is the recency count form. This is what the report would look like.
It will simply tell you how many people fall into each of these recency categories. This
many people bought in the last 30 days, this many people in 60 days. When you look at
this you start noticing things.

-- 15 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
For example, you’ve got 71,000 total customers. You notice if you don’t do something
quick you’re going to lose 8,000 of them in the next 30 days, another 5,400 in the next 30
days and another 5,000 in the next. Altogether that’s 18,000 people who are no longer
going to be active customers if you don’t do something to activate them.
Immediately what does that say? I’ve got to mount some kind of a special campaign to get
as many of these people as possible to make a purchase right away.
What does that mean? Well, it may mean a special offer or customer appreciation item. An
anniversary with the company, “You’ve been with us eight months – or nine months -- and
I just want to thank you for being a great customers. Here’s a special discount on product
XYZ.”
The next thing you’ll notice are some lumps in here. You’ll find that you’ve got this big
rabbit going through this snake here at 211 to 240 days. This number is going to be here
this month, the month after that and so forth, until they fall off the file. There may be
something you want to do with these people.
You can also notice the number of people coming in over the last 90 days. Those who
have made a purchase in the last 90 days is somewhat smaller than the number of people
who are about to fall off the file. You’ll learn a lot about this.
If you take this form and on this column you make this January’s quantity, then February’s
and March and update it every single month, you begin seeing trends in every one of
these categories going horizontally. That will show you if your purchase quantity in the last
30 days was higher or lower.
You’ll be able to see how each of these categories is changing. You’ll be able to see
opportunities and challenges on the file and head them off at the pass or capitalize on
them. You’d never even have known these existed without this report.
This is just the recency report. You will also have one for frequency that will show the
same information. You will have one for each of the amount categories. That gives you
enormous power in terms of creating the strategy for promoting to that house file.
Let’s look at some rules for promoting to a house file. Like everything with me, rules tend
to be rules of thumb. At least consider them. They have worked very well for me.
Rule One
When I’m working with a new client, and especially copywriters who are writing for me for
that client, we have four crucial goals that we want to accomplish in every promotion we
sent to the house file.

-- 16 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
The first one is to strengthen the bond between the customer and the company. If you go
in with bombastic sales copy, over promising, threatening, or insulting just to make the
sale, you may make the sale this month but you may have lost the customer forever.
If you send a customer an abusive number of promotions, especially a customer who has
asked not to be sent a large number of promotions, you may make a sale today but you’re
spending moral capital. At some point down the road this is going to adversely affect his
lifetime value to your company. He’ll either begin ordering less or drop out altogether.
On the other hand, if each promotion has as a main objective to make the customer
feel closer to and more trusting of the company and more in love with your products or
services, now you’ve taken a big step towards extending customer lifetime value. We saw
what that does in terms of increasing the value of the company.
The next objective with every promotion is to acquire a new piece of information about the
customer. We just talked about that. That gives you enormous marketing power down the
road.
The third thing is to upgrade the customer in some way, in terms of frequency, recency or
amount.
Finally, of course, is to produce maximum revenues. Revenues come last. If you product
maximum revenues but you make the customer eager not to hear from you again, you’ve
blown it.
It’s really important to say that. There are so many people who are so intent on this
month’s sales that they are unwilling to leave any money on the table. Sometimes leaving
a little money on, or even giving away a free gift with no ulterior motive, is the way to make
sure that customer’s will order next month and the next and the next.
Just like any relationship, at some point the customer adds up the pluses and minuses
and decides whether or not he wants to continue it. You want always to come out on the
positive side of that equation. Every contact with a customer is an opportunity to bond, sell
and gather information.
A lot of companies, especially those run by bean counters, are looking for opportunities not
to communicate with customers. They’re looking for opportunities to cut costs by reducing
the number of things that you send to customers. Like most of the things bean counters
do, it’s idiotic and upside down.
Instead, what you need to be looking for are more ways to contact the customer, more
excuses to write to him, call him, or send an email.

-- 17 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
I forget who it was but I saw on Michel Fortin’s blog somebody had come across one of my
clients and bought a product from him. In the package with his first product he got a free
calendar and a bunch of coupons for discounts on future purchases. He got a very nice,
personal, warm letter from the owner. He got 20 or 30 pieces of paper in that packet.
A bean counter would say, “Your outgoing packages are too expensive.” However, the
materials in that package bonds people to us. They are much more likely to come back
and make a secondary purchase 30, 60, 90 days down the road. It’s because they got all
that nice warm, fuzzy stuff, an added value they didn’t expect.
You look for opportunities to contact the customer and to spend money through contacting
customers.
You have your normal outgoing promotions. We’re going to look at what a typical monthly
promotion might look like in a moment.
Receipt and thank you letters. A lot of people will forget this, but if somebody buys
something from you and it’s going to be a couple of days before you ship, why not send
them a first class letter or email thanking them for their purchase.
In it either ask them to fill out a short survey or poll, or have a little stuffer for a product that
they can add to their order at a favorable price. These always pay for themselves if they’re
done right.
Product shipment and packaging, we just talked about that. You’re sending the package
anyway. It costs you very little in postage or shipping costs to include other materials that
bring value to the customer and therefore bond him to the company.
Customer inquiries and complaints. Again, the telephone sales force is extremely
important here. Getting with them and finding out how they handled these things is
valuable on so many levels.
Number one, you can find out what the complaints are. That is valuable as testimonials in
terms of crafting future promotions. Scripting those people so that every time they talk to a
customer they get a piece of information you can use, whether it’s a complaint, inquiry, or
whatever.
Script them so they give away something free. If somebody calls in to complain that an
order was mixed up or something, have a list of three or four things you can send the
person to apologize.
“Gee, I’m sorry we messed up that order. We never do that kind of thing. Please accept

-- 18 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
this as a token of my sincere regret.” Have a free special report or gift of some kind that
costs you very little.
Look for opportunities to do opinion polls and surveys. People love sharing their opinions.
Sometimes even if the answers to the poll aren’t necessarily helpful to you, but just the fact
that you demonstrated your concern for the customer by asking for their opinion causes
bonding to take place.
The Product First vitamin supplement industry. Product First means you send out a
promotion selling a supplement and you’re making a direct supplement sale. You’re not
selling a newsletter first and then they’re buying something the doctor recommended.
With Product First promotions you’ve got a product, you send out a bunch of direct mail
pieces, and you get a whole bunch of new customers.
One of the problems that companies doing this are having is that their customers don’t
know who the company is. They buy a lot of health products. They buy their vitamins at
the grocery store. They buy their minerals at the drug store. They’ll buy something from
Healthy Direction and something from Health Resources. Something else will be bought
from Nature’s Way.
They’re not bonded to the company. They may not even know who the company is. They
know what the product is that they are looking forward to receiving but the company has
no visibility in their mind.
Going to them with a promotion where you ask their opinion in a poll or a survey raises the
visibility of your company. It increases the chances that customer is going to remember
you and remember the company when you go back to him with another promotion.
Typically customer file promotions to those kinds of customer lists yield very low response
rates. The reason is they are not aware of the company. They know they ordered the
product but they’re not bonding. This is a good way to do that.
Value added gifts. There’s nothing that has the impact of ordering a product and getting
a couple of unadvertised bonuses in the product. We love calendars. We love to do a
calendar with the owner and his family: his mom, dad, kids and everybody else on the
cover. Every month on the calendar there’s a coupon for a discount on one of the client’s
products.
It needs to be something the customer is going to want to keep. It’s got to be big and
beautiful, something they’re going to want to tack up on the wall or stick on the refrigerator.
That type of thing has a lot of benefit to you.

-- 19 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page 0 of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Not only will the coupons in the calendar generate more sales than you would normally
get, but being able to see the company owner and his family he becomes a real person.
He becomes part of your family, not just a salesman that wants to get his hand in your
wallet.
Company newsletters are also great. We’ll look at how we use those in a moment.
Then events like this one. Something like this is great. It takes a day or two to put together
a Power Point for a webinar, then an hour or hour and a half in this case I guess to actually
do the webinar.
It has tremendous value for your customer. The cost is nominal but it brings a value to
people’s lives and creates a very strong bond with the customer.
Rule Three
Be willing to spend at least as much to keep a customer as you’re willing to pay to get a
customer.
I was talking to Drayton Bird last week about this. He’s doing database management. He’s
doing just this kind of thing right now for Richard Branson at Virgin Atlantic. He’s dealing
with very savvy business people and multi-billion dollar companies. He has the same
battle. The companies are not structured to keep customers. They’re structured to go out
and get customers. But if a customer is leaving the fold they don’t have a plan for that or
allocate a budget for that.
It only makes sense to be willing to spend at least as much to keep a customer as you’re
willing to pay to get one. Think about it. It’s all right here on this slide. Response rates are
typically 8 to 10 times higher when you’re selling to someone who has bought from you
before as it is when you’re selling to cold prospects, rented lists.
The average sale is typically higher, too, sometimes twice as high. Getting new customers
requires capital risk. To do a mailing of a million pieces it costs half a million dollars.
You put that money out there and what if a new war starts or a terrorist event or the stock
market crashes. Or what if OJ offs another ex-wife? All of the sudden people are glued
to their TVs, they’re not reading their mail, and your response falls through the floor. That
whole half a million dollars goes down the drain.
I’ve seen it happen. That money is at risk. It doesn’t matter how well proven the promotion
is. I’ve seen the promotion go from 100% of cost ROI down to 20% just because of some
unforeseen national news event, so getting new customers involves significant capital risk.

-- 20 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
But the higher response and higher average sale to your customer file covers you. Even if
the worst happens you’re not going to lose your money on the mailing. Those customers
are very important.
Smart marketers are willing to pay at least four times more to keep one if your average
customer is with you for seven years, which is about the average that we find over time.
If you’re doing better than that you’re fantastic. If you’re doing less than that it’s kind of a
good thing. If you’re client is doing less than that, it’s a good thing. Now you get to be the
hero that gets them to seven years or maybe even a little more.
But if your average customer is with you seven years and spends an average of $500 a
year, losing that average customer will cost you $3,500, so how much are you willing to
spend of that $3,500 to make him happy?
Give him a refund. Let him keep the product. For $3,500 I’d be willing to spend three
minutes on the phone to apologize to a customer. What’s it going to take?
Rule Four
First impressions mean a lot. This goes back to customer acquisition as well. Let’s just
think this through.
Step one, when you’re contacting a prospect, he’s not yet a customer. This is the time to
start the bonding process. Again bonding equals customer lifetime value. Bonding equals
longevity. As long as he’s with you he’s going to be buying. Therefore, bonding equals
lifetime value. That’s the goal of all of this.
When you’re doing your initial promotion you want personal messaging. People don’t
bond with companies they bond with people. So you have personal messaging from the
president of the company or from your figurehead or advocate.
You establish the company position as being superior in some way to the competition. And
you create trust, a big part of that really, in the promotion we would be doing anyway, in
terms of credibility devices, testimonials, and other things.
But trust extends beyond just the promotion piece. It also extends to Step 2, which is the
actual buying experience. To have the people who answer the phone not to be some of
these slugs who I’ve heard when I’ve called in to order something from an infomercial or
some other kind of promotion.
They need to be warm. They need to be friendly. They need to be trained to handle the
customer and to treat them like a king. They need not to be abusive. This is one of those
areas where you have an opportunity to make a sale and make sure, at the same time,

-- 21 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
that customer will never buy another blessed thing from you as long as he lives.
If you’ve ever responded to an infomercial on TV you know what I’m talking about. You call
in to buy that $19 product and the customer service rep beats the holy crap out of you until
you buy five other things, and then he wants to charge you $35 for FedEx shipping. That
kind of thing basically guarantees that customer will never come back to you again.
You set up the service, the telephone reps, in a way so that they handle that customer
with kid gloves and make him feel like he’s joining a family. You turn your product around
fast. There’s a personal thank you letter from the president. If you don’t want to send it
separately as a first class letter (which I would do), fine. Stick it in the product package.
Put it in a nice envelope. Personalize the envelope. Personalize the letter. Deliver an
unadvertised bonus.
And then this next one is really important. If the new customer is coming on your file for
the first time, don’t immediately throw him into the normal promotional cycle that you’re
doing to your customer file. Create a new customer experience.
It can be a week long, two weeks long, I’ve done them up to 90 days long, where every
new customer that comes in goes through a very careful process whereby he is given
tremendous value, he’s bonded with the company, and he’s prepared, prior to being put
into the regular promotional cycle that you have at your company.
But just as a quickie, I have a client for example that has PPC campaigns on the web
that draw you in if you’re searching for advice on international investing, and another
PPC campaign that draws you in if you’re searching for information or advice on currency
investing.
Well, we’re creating one experience for the people who are interested in foreign investing
and a completely different experience for the ones who have told us what they’re
interested in is currency investing.
The goal of both these programs is to stay with the subject that they’ve told us that they’re
interested in and have a product ladder that moves them from new opt-in to the ezine to
their first $29 product offered, to their $49, and then to their $99, and by the time they get
to that point they’re ready to join the file where we’re offering products that range in price
from $1,000 to $25,000.
So think through that process. Think how your customer came to you, what he told you
about his interests, by responding to your first offer to him. And think how you can then
continue to deliver or address that interest or that concern, that fear, frustration or desire,
in that first period of time on file.

-- 22 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page 3 of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
There are two kinds of promotions typically. Now we’re getting down to planning your
monthly promotion strategy. There are two kinds of promotions generally that you’ll do to a
file.
The first type is horizontal promotions. We call them that because they cut across the
file, based on a shared qualifications of a particular segment. For example, a horizontal
promotion might target a section of the file based on their recency, frequency or one of the
amount categories we’ve identified.
You might say, “I want to mail a promotion this month to everybody who has a birthday this
month, or everybody who has an anniversary this month, or all the guys, or all the gals, or
all the people between 30-40, or 40-50, or all the people in Dayton, Ohio, or all the people
who have been with me a year or more, or all of the people who have placed a recent
order.”
Then there are vertical promotions that target the entire file and are triggered by shared
events. Imagine you’ve got a big file. This is cutting layers off the cake. This is drilling down
through the cake and going to the whole file. Holidays and red letter days, Christmas,
Easter, New Years, Hanukkah, Kwanzaa, 4th of July – holidays and red letter days.
Breaking news events – We get a lot of this in the financial area. “On Friday, President
Bush and Ben Bernanke both went to great pains to say that they’re going to spend as
much of my money as is necessary to bail out lenders who made risky real estate loans.”
That is a breaking news event that to a financial file is very important, so that promotion
would go to the entire file.
New product announcements – If you have a new product and if the price point is
appropriate for your entire file (we’ll talk about that in a second), then you may want to go
vertical with that and go to the entire file.
Now sometimes, like for example we just launched two products on currencies. One of
them is a $2,500 product and the other one is a $900 product. The reason we did that
was that they’re both very different products, but they both focus on the same concept:
currencies.
So we’re taking the people who have bought more with us, our best recency, frequency
and amount people, and they’re receiving the promotions for the $2,500 service. We’re
taking the rest of the file and they’re getting the promotion for the lower price service.
That’s possible to do if you have that kind of price variation.
So those are two kinds of promotion: horizontal and vertical. The point is you have a
month laying out before you like a great banquet in which you are about to take part, right?
And you have this wonderful customer file sitting there, with all this money in their pockets,

-- 23 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
and every one of them is a bundle of needs for the products that you have.
Now the question is how do you get the right offers to the right people? How do you make
sure that you do some upgrading on your file every month, that people are upgraded on
the basis of recency, frequency and amount every single month?
Well, you lay out a schedule that might look something like this. Again, this is just to give
you the concept. You’re going to have to think your way through this, and it will be different
for every client that you work with, but this one is fairly typical.
We have an e-letter for this particular client. He will let us run ads in that e-letter. That
ezine goes out Monday through Saturday in the morning, and people can order either by
phone or the shopping cart.
This is going to be a horizontal promotion. In other words, we’re going to stratify the list.
We’re going to have higher priced products for our higher priced people and we’re going to
have lower priced products for our lower priced people.
But we’re going to stretch them a little bit, and we’re going to skew it so that we’re offering
$200 products to people whose largest purchase was $150 until now, so we’re upgrading
them by price. That’s going to be our e-letter plan for this month.
The products may change, but that’s the basic strategy. Then in the afternoons every day
except Sunday we’re going to send an email promo. Again, the sales device is the phone
or the shopping cart. Again it’s horizontal; we’re going to stratify the file.
These can be countdowns; they can be whatever you want them to be. But again, the
point here is to be thinking about who is going to receive each message and making sure
that they’re being upgraded in some way.
Then we have our print newsletter that goes out on the first Friday of the month. People
can order from an insert in that newsletter, either by phone or return mail. This is a print
piece. If we want to get real fancy, we can make this a horizontal and upgrade by price.
Typically though it would be vertical. Everybody would receive the same offer.
We would select a product at a price point that would be attractive to the greatest number
of people in our file. Then on the second Friday of the month, we would do a standalone
direct mail promo. This would be a personalized letter, a postcard, any kind of format we
want to do, but it would be highly personal and it would go to the entire file.
It could be horizontal or vertical, depending on what we want to do, whether we want this
to be an upgrade, whether we want this to match our products to the people who are most
likely to buy them, which is basically a revenue maximizer, or we can just do a vertical and
everyone gets the same offer.

-- 24 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Then on the third Friday everyone gets a magalog essentially, that we call the company
magazine. It has a lot of value-added editorial that makes people love it and want to read
it, and some of the articles culminate in a product offer and are also ads for the product.
We can have various products in that and it’s totally vertical. Everyone gets the same
magazine.
Finally, on the fourth Friday we have another standalone promo. It’s a very different format
from the one that they received two weeks earlier, and this can be horizontal or vertical,
either way, your call. In this case, I’m saying segment by the largest sale. It could be
recency, frequency, whatever you want to upgrade on.
There’s another kind of promotional schedule you can look at. That’s when you’re
launching a new product. Just very quickly going through that, this is pretty self
explanatory.
Basically when you launch a new product, you want to have an introductory price. You
want to offer them the best discount they’ll ever get if they buy this product now. You want
to have a deadline when that offer is going to end.
And when you have a deadline, that means that you can have a countdown to the
deadline. You can be reminding people that if they don’t order now, they only have
three days left, two days left, one day left. Very effective. And this is what one of those
campaigns might look like. You can go through it, it’s self explanatory. But you use all of
the same devices.
In your multimedia, you’re using ezines, email promos, print newsletter, stand alone DM.
And just so I’m really clear here, stand alone DM can be a personal letter any size format.
It can be anything you want it to be, but because this is your customer list, it should be as
personal as possible.
In this case, everything is focused on the countdown, and the new product, the countdown
and the offer.
Here are some tips to think about as you’re designing these campaigns. First, in direct
mail, it almost always pays to use first class postage. That’s for several reasons.
Number one, believe it or not, there are people who notice that when they receive letters
from you, they come first class. You invested the time and money to send them a letter, to
go first class with them. For the ones who are a little dull and don’t notice it right away, I
like to stick a big old red stamp on the outside of the envelope that says First Class Mail. It
says something about how you value them as a customer.

-- 25 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
It also gives you your buying data back faster. Instead of a two-week delivery time that you
would get with bulk mail, which slows down your entire campaign on your house file, you
get delivery in three days. Some of your people get next day delivery on a first class, who
are close by the mail center that you use.
So first class on customer house file promotions is just really important, because it
gives you the kind of timing accuracy you need in order to have the number of customer
contacts you need to conduct an optimal house file promotion campaign.
Second, heavily personalizing communications with customers pays dividends. I don’t
mean just saying “Dear Mr. Flintstone” at the beginning. Remember, you have a customer
record and you know a lot more about the guy. At the very least you can say, “I hope things
are going great for you there in Bedrock” or “Has it been a hot summer in Bedrock?” or
“How’s old Wilma doing?” This is where a great customer record really pays dividends.
I’ve told the story before of how (and this is outside the customer record), we wanted to
know how many savings & loans and banks in each customer’s area, within a 30 mile
radius of their house, were in trouble. This was back during the S&L crisis. We were able
to find a database that told us that.
All the database told us was which institutions were in trouble and where they were
located. Then by zip code matching, we were able to say, “Hey, you know that savings &
loan down on the corner of Such and Such and So and So? Well, it’s in big trouble.” We
were able to tell them a little bit about it.
We did another one that you’ve probably seen where we matched an AAA report on
gasoline prices and we were able to tell people that the gas station down on Such and
Such an address in their town was charging this much for gas.
Those kinds of promotions get a lot of attention and response rockets when you can get
that kind of personalization into your promotions.
Third, keeping a variety of formats going. We’ve talked about that earlier in this call. We’ve
used postcards from France, we’ve used magalogs, and we’ve used envelopes that look
like FedEx envelopes. We’ve used envelopes that look like invoices, envelopes that look
like checks.
Constantly be changing the format. When you go to them with the same old look all the
time, some people say “Well, I’ve seen this before,” and it just gets trashed. They don’t
even open it. Keeping it interesting, keeping it lively, creating a fascinating tableau keeps
your response high, no matter how many times you contact these people.
Giving your customers non-promotional gifts once a quarter or twice a year or just before

-- 26 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
the holidays is also great. We talked about the unadvertised bonus, but this is more than
that. Maybe they didn’t even order it, but they get something from you of value.
It amazes me that so few people use coupons, for example. A discount coupon is just an
absolutely wonderful way of saying “I love you” to a customer. Send them a 10% discount
as a “just because” gift in February for Valentine’s Day. “This is my love letter.”
You don’t have to get sappy about it, but you can say “I’ve been thinking about all of the
things that I have to be grateful for, and that’s when I thought of you, John. I just wanted to
send you this discount. No promotion, no strings, just here’s a coupon. Use it any way you
want. It’s my way of saying thanks for everything you’ve done for me and for our company
over the last year.”
And you don’t think the phones will ring when you send that out? You don’t have to tell
them what to do with it. They know how to use a coupon. So look for opportunities to bond
in those kinds of ways. It pays dividends.
Offering customer rewards credits for purchases provides many more opportunities for
profitable customer contacts. This takes some doing. It takes setting up the IT and there is
some coordination. But you know, the airlines have frequent flier miles for a reason. They
know that it’s going to bring you back. It brings you to the airline in the first place to earn
the miles, and it brings you back again to build up your balances.
The same thing is true if you’re selling supplements or many other kinds of products. If
someone is buying something from you that they need 5-6 times a year, making sure that
they get it from you and not from someone else might be as easy as giving them a vested
interest in your company. Every purchase they make is worth so much toward their next
purchase, or some other kind of rewards program.
And you might also try an Inner Circle offer. This is good for mature companies. I have one
client where the client offers a bunch of different services, and they range in price all over
the map, from as little as $29 up to $5,000.
So we set up two Inner Circles for him; one for the lower echelon buyers, and one for the
higher echelon buyers. The first one sells for $15,000 and the second sells for $25,000.
Once you join this, you get all of his services for life, forever. With the $25,000 service you
get everything the company produces; for $15,000 you get everything he produces, with
his name on it as author. We sold millions of dollars worth of those.
So consider Inner Circle when you have a company that has a significant number of
names at the top, a significant number of high frequency, high average purchase names at
the top – people who buy just about everything you offer them. Then it’s time to seriously
consider one of these Inner Circle things. In another call we’ll talk about that in more detail

-- 27 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
and I’ll show you how I do it.
Here is the consultant’s guide to get going on setting up this kind of a program in five
simple steps.
1. 	Identify what really is a customer.
We talked about that. An active customer is someone who has ordered within the last 12
months in most cases.
Make sure each record contains all valuable data on each customer that you have.
I’ve gone into companies where they have some of the data on their database about a
customer -- for example, they’ll have all of his transactional history, but they won’t have
any information on where he came from in the first place, or they won’t have information
on there about any refund requests that he’s made.
So go through the database records. That information was on paper somewhere in the
company, and we had to import it into the database. Make sure that every scrap of
information you have on every customer is on the customer records.
2. 	Ask the question.
You don’t have to be a computer genius and I’m certainly not, but ask the question. Go to
whoever knows and say “Hey, look, if I want to mail just to the 50 year old guys who have
bought within the last three months and spent more than $200, and over their lifetime have
spent more than $2,000, and if I ask you something that specific, can you do it? And if so,
how long will it take?”
Chances are most people will say, “Yeah, we can do it.” But what they won’t tell you is that
it will probably take them six weeks to figure that out. So if the software isn’t set up to be
able to quickly and very efficiently be able to segment the file, you have some work to do
there.
I have to say that when I went to work at Blanchard, they were in that kind of situation
and it took us six months to be able to get our first reports that told us what our average
lifetime value was. It’s worth the work, it’s worth the time. You can still be promoting in the
meantime to the house file, but getting this information up front is very important.
3. 	Establish your benchmarks.
This is before you appeared on the scene. You have all of this data now, so you’re going
to print some reports and you’re going to establish how many names are in each RFA
category, like the reports we looked at.

-- 28 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page  of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
You’re going to look at the recency/frequency amount for the average customer. That’s
very different. This tells you how many names are in each recency, frequency and
amount category. This tells you that across the entire company, the average recency of
order is three months. The average frequency is six times a year. The average amount
of purchase is $X. The largest purchase among all of our customers is this amount. It’s a
very different report, very important to know.
By the way, one of the reasons that is important is because it establishes where you are
today. It allows you to set goals for each of these categories for tomorrow, and then see
the longevity and lifetime value of the average customer.
This may take awhile, but this is the Holy Grail. This is what everything else you’re doing
leads up to: extending longevity and increasing lifetime value. After having done that, now
you have a clear picture of who your customers are and what your file looks like.
4. 	The next step is to identify your objectives.
You need to study those benchmark reports to determine the most pressing challenges
and the biggest opportunities on those reports.
We talked about looking at the cradle to grave idea; when they’re born, how they’re
handled, what could be done better. The opportunity there is tremendous, because a lot of
new customers become non-customers, although you don’t know it, in those first 30 days,
because of the way they’re handled, so look at the beginning and ending of their lifetimes
first.
Then look in between to identify particular groups on the file, by recency, frequency and
amount that seem to be presenting you with the greatest opportunities. This will also really
help you in terms of product development and pricing, for obvious reasons.
If you find that your customers are ten times more likely to buy a $49 product than they are
a $300 product, then obviously you’re going to make more money with a $49 product. You
may find the opposite is true. But look for those kinds of opportunities on the file.
5. 	Then create your promotion plan.
Create spacing between each customer contact. Now in email, daily contact is just fine,
even twice a day if the contacts are very different is okay. Any more than that, you’re going
to get a lot of complaints and opt-outs.
In the mail, though, weekly is certainly fine. I’ve gone as often as three times a week
and not seen any erosion at all in customer longevity. Actually, the more visible you are

-- 29 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page 30 of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
– remember the old saying, “Out of sight, out of mind”?
If you’re constantly in their face, you’re going to be top of mind for them. If you’re not there,
they’re not going to be thinking about you. If you are there, you’re going to find that your
response rates rise, and in some cases your average sale as well.
Create your promotion plan, and make sure that you have specialized promotions every
month designed to upgrade weaker file segments, capitalize on stronger ones, and
maximize revenue from the overall file.
Then it’s time to execute. And that’s when you get back to copywriting.
Jeff says “Obviously a big limiting factor is the ability of the company.” Let me talk about
that a little bit. Jeff says that “A limiting factor is the ability of the company to keep ginning
up new products that are good.”
There are a lot of limiting factors of the companies that you’re going to work with. Let’s
just talk about a few of those. How do you know that a particular company would be a
good candidate for you to get into this kind of business with, to go in and set up house file
marketing?
Well, number one, there needs to be a level of trust. There needs to be people that you
can trust to tell you the truth about what their revenues are. I receive sales reports from my
clients four times a day. I know minute by minute almost – hour by hour, certainly – during
the working day how well my promotions are working. I have a long enough relationship
with this client that I know I can trust them. Trust is very important.
One of the worst wastes of time you’ll ever get into is crawling into bed with somebody that
is just flighty, someone that’s going to end up messing with you and costing you money.
It’s so disappointing when that happens. Fortunately, I haven’t had that happen a lot of
times in my life. I think a lot of it was luck and some of it was just that I tend to be very
picky when choosing clients.
Another qualification a company has to have in order to be a good fit for this kind of a deal
is that the company has to want to grow aggressively. The company owner has got to be
willing to invest in infrastructure to support what you’re going to do. I once told a client that
I feel like I’m a 4000 horsepower dragster motor that someone just strapped onto a roller
skate.
The company didn’t have a marketing department that could support the kind of
promotions that we needed to do. It takes qualified personnel to get all of this material
to the customers, to stratify the file, to do all of the selects, and to get things moving. So
the company owner with whom you’re going to do business needs to be someone that’s

-- 30 of 31 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 4 • Makepeace Money Multiplier
Transcript 	Page 3 of 3
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
willing to do that kind of investment in his own company.
When you go to talk to the people at the company, you need to make sure that they
feel open and excited about what you all are about to do together. If you get the feeling
that they are guarding their turf, and they really don’t want you there, then that’s a major
problem that you should discuss with the company owner.
I had an experience once where I spent six weeks at a client’s offices reorganizing his
company for him, and we structured a reporting system, we structured everything from the
ground up – the company structure, reporting structure, everything, to create just a kick
butt online marketing department.
The minute I left, his employees started going to the owner and saying “Well, I don’t really
want to do this report,” or “It would just take too long,” or “I don’t want to report to so and
so” and the owner caved on almost every point. The end result was that I gave up six
weeks with my family to do this work with a client and nothing was implemented.
So you need to have a level of confidence that the work you’re going to be doing with the
client is going to be used. That’s important. And yes, Jeff, the ability to create products
goes in with all of that.
Okay, guys. I want to thank you for your time today. Either I’ve boggled your minds or
you’re so busy taking notes that you don’t have time to ask more questions.
Thanks for everything. Take care.

-- 31 of 31 --
